content_en:
Few books in the Islamic scholarly tradition have been as widely transmitted, annotated, and taught as Tafsir al-Jalalain. From the moment al-Suyuti completed his portion in the late ninth century of the Islamic calendar, the work entered the curriculum of traditional Islamic education and has never left it. Understanding the work's transmission history helps explain both its authority and its proper place in the student's journey.

Al-Suyuti himself narrated the completed work to students before his death in 911 AH. These students, in turn, transmitted it with their own ijazat (permissions to teach), and the text spread rapidly across the Islamic world — to the Ottoman lands, to the Maghreb, to sub-Saharan Africa, to the Indian subcontinent, and to Southeast Asia. Each region developed its own tradition of teaching the text, often alongside local supercommentaries that expanded on points al-Jalalain left compressed.

The most famous supercommentary is Hashiyat al-Sawi ala Tafsir al-Jalalain by Imam Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Sawi (d. 1241 AH / 1825 CE), an Egyptian Maliki scholar. Al-Sawi's hashiya adds narrations, jurisprudential notes, grammatical alternatives, and theological elaborations that the main text deliberately omitted. Students in the Maliki tradition of West Africa and the Maghreb often study al-Sawi's hashiya alongside al-Jalalain as a standard set.

Another important commentary is the hashiya of Shaykh Ibrahim al-Bayjuri (d. 1276 AH / 1860 CE), the Egyptian Shafi'i scholar who also wrote major glosses on al-Sanusiyyah (the creed primer) and al-Burdah. Al-Bayjuri's gloss on al-Jalalain is widely used in Shafi'i educational circles, particularly in Egypt and the broader Arab world.

The work has been printed in numerous editions, the most common being the two-column layout that places the Quranic text in the center with the commentary text interleaved in a different font or marked by brackets, allowing the reader to follow both simultaneously. Modern editions from Dar al-Hadith, Dar al-Fikr, and other major publishers have standardized the text and made it globally available.

Translations of al-Jalalain are available in multiple languages, though the work's value is primarily realized in Arabic — the grammatical and lexical dimension of the commentary is inseparable from the Arabic text it illuminates. English translations exist for reference purposes, but students are generally advised that the real benefit of the work is gained only through Arabic study.

In the twenty-first century, Tafsir al-Jalalain remains a foundational text of traditional Islamic education. Digital editions, audio recordings of scholars reciting and explaining the text, and online platforms have extended its reach. It occupies a position analogous to a grammar primer in secular education: the gateway through which every serious student of the Quran must pass before advancing to deeper study.
